vertebra.title: The Whale Vertebra
vertebra.description: When a whale dies and sinks, its skeleton becomes a nutrient source that sustains deep-sea life for decades. This vertebra was found at 3,895 m depth, during a late stage of ecological succession. The bone is slowly mineralizing back into the seafloor, and its hard surface provides an anchor point for corals and other sessile organisms in an environment dominated by soft sediments.
vertebra.caption: Clip: Moment of extraction - R/V Falkor (too)

isopods.title: The Isopod Train
isopods.description: These tiny crustaceans, each under 2 cm, were observed lined up along a branch of coral attached to the whale bone. They are part of a specialized benthic community adapted to the high-pressure, low-energy conditions of the deep South Atlantic.
isopods.caption: In-situ macrophotography

subastian.title: ROV SuBastian
subastian.description: SuBastian is the ROV used to explore and sample these sites. The 3D model you are navigating is a digital twin built from photogrammetry — thousands of high-resolution images processed under the lead of pilot Ben Erwin. SuBastian's robotic arms enable precise collection of fragile biological samples with minimal disturbance to the surrounding ecosystem.

lab.title: From Seafloor to Lab
lab.description: Once a sample reaches the surface, it is immediately moved to the Cold Room. A multidisciplinary team handles recovery and analysis; the Benthic Ecology and Taxonomy teams then work to identify species and study deep-sea biodiversity, keeping everything in cold storage at +2 °C.
lab.thumb1: José Isola and Daniel Lauretta handling the vertebra after recovery.
lab.thumb3: Detail of the bone structure in the laboratory.

mission.title: Expedition FKt251206
mission.description: Led by María Emilia Bravo, the FKt251206 campaign aimed to describe and classify the broad diversity of deep-sea benthic landscapes and ecosystems in the Argentine Sea. This included identifying priority areas for future research and taking environmental management actions.
